mnemonics.title: Group Properties
mnemonics.content: C-A-I-I: Closure, Associativity, Identity, Inverses - Remember 'Clever Algebraists Invented Integers'

mnemonics.title: Abelian Group
mnemonics.content: A-B-E-L-I-A-N: 'Always Both Elements Leave Any Note In A commutAive maNner' - Commutative property

mnemonics.title: Lagrange's Theorem
mnemonics.content: 'Ladies In Genie Love Having Older Patrons' - Order of Subgroup divides Order of Group (|H| | |G|)

mnemonics.title: Subgroup Test
mnemonics.content: 'Never Eat Shredded Wheat' - Non-empty, a,b∈H implies ab⁻¹∈H

acronyms.expansion: Group - set with binary operation satisfying four properties
acronyms.usage: Denotes a mathematical group in group theory
Element that doesn't change other elements when combined: e*a = a*e = a
Element that combined with a gives identity: a*a⁻¹ = a⁻¹*a = e
Number of elements in group G (cardinality)
Set of all powers of a: {aⁿ : n ∈ Z}
Set of elements commuting with all elements in G

visualTips.visualization: Think of a clock - moving around positions by adding hours, eventually returning to start. Z₁₂ is like a 12-hour clock.
Think of nested circles - the subgroup is a smaller circle inside the larger group circle, with same center (identity).
Picture a puzzle - the group is partitioned into equal-sized pieces (cosets), each piece being the size of the subgroup.
Imagine spinning a wheel - how many spins to return to original position? That's the order.

analogies.analogy: Like a machine with input-output: combine two elements get third. Similar to combining ingredients in cooking.
Like 0 in addition or 1 in multiplication - the 'do nothing' element that leaves others unchanged.
Like opposite directions: forward/back, up/down, positive/negative - returns to starting point.
Like hours on a clock - 12 steps bring you back to where you started. Same position can be reached by different numbers of steps.
Like a team within a larger organization - follows same rules, has same structure but fewer members.
Like adding money: $5 + $3 = $3 + $5 = $8. The order doesn't matter.
